stem: Use an integrating factor to solve the linear first-order IVP \( \dfrac{dy}{dx} + [[p]]y = [[q]] \), \( y(0) = [[y0]] \), and evaluate the solution at \( x = [[t0]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the wrong sign in the exponent of the transient term.
This forgets the steady-state term \( q/p \) entirely.
This flips the sign of the transient's coefficient.
This treats the forcing as producing linear rather than exponential-approach behavior.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.

stem: Using the integrating factor \( \mu(x)=x^{[[n]]} \), solve the linear IVP \( \dfrac{dy}{dx} + \dfrac{[[n]]}{x}y = x^{[[m]]} \), \( y([[x0]]) = [[y0]] \), and evaluate the solution at \( x = [[t0]] \).
solution: Multiplying by \( \mu(x)=x^{[[n]]} \) makes the left side \( (x^{[[n]]}y)' \), so \( x^{[[n]]}y = \dfrac{x^{[[n]]+[[m]]+1}}{[[n]]+[[m]]+1}+C \). Using \( y([[x0]])=[[y0]] \) to find \( C \) and evaluating at \( x=[[t0]] \) gives \( y([[t0]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the homogeneous (constant-of-integration) term entirely.
This uses a pure power-law ratio, ignoring the particular-solution term from the forcing.
This subtracts the homogeneous term instead of adding it.
This forgets to divide by the integrating factor before evaluating.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.

stem: The forcing rate \( [[k]] \) does NOT match the equation's own decay rate, so solve the non-resonant linear IVP \( \dfrac{dy}{dx} + [[p]]y = e^{[[k]]x} \), \( y(0) = [[y0]] \), and evaluate the solution at \( x = [[t0]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to subtract the transient's contribution from the particular solution's initial value.
This uses the wrong sign in the exponent of the homogeneous term.
This drops the forced response entirely, keeping only the homogeneous solution.
This drops the homogeneous solution entirely, keeping only the particular solution.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.

stem: Solve the linear IVP \( \dfrac{dy}{dx} + [[p]]y = \cos([[k]]x) \), \( y(0) = [[y0]] \), and evaluate the solution at \( x = \dfrac{[[m]]\pi}{2\cdot[[k]]} \).
solution: Undetermined coefficients (or the integrating factor) gives the particular solution \( y_p = \dfrac{[[k]]\sin([[k]]x)+[[p]]\cos([[k]]x)}{[[k]]^{2}+[[p]]^{2}} \), so \( y(x) = y_p(x) + Ce^{-[[p]]x} \) with \( C = [[y0]] - y_p(0) \). Evaluating at \( x = \dfrac{[[m]]\pi}{2\cdot[[k]]} \) gives \( y = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This swaps the roles of sine and cosine in the particular solution.
This drops the homogeneous (transient) term entirely.
This drops the particular (forced) response entirely, keeping only the transient.
This uses the wrong sign in the exponent of the transient term.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.

stem: A tank holds \( [[vol]] \) liters of brine. Fluid with salt concentration \( [[cin]] \) kg/L flows in at \( [[r]] \) L/min, and the well-mixed solution drains out at the same rate, so the amount of salt \( Q(t) \) (kg) satisfies the linear equation \( \dfrac{dQ}{dt} + \dfrac{[[r]]}{[[vol]]}Q = [[r]]\cdot[[cin]] \). If \( Q(0) = [[q0]] \), find \( Q([[t0]]) \).
solution: The integrating factor is \( e^{([[r]]/[[vol]])t} \), giving \( Q(t) = [[cin]]\cdot[[vol]] + Ce^{-([[r]]/[[vol]])t} \). Since \( Q(0)=[[q0]] \), \( C=[[q0]]-[[cin]]\cdot[[vol]] \), so \( Q([[t0]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the wrong sign in the exponent, growing away from equilibrium instead of decaying toward it.
This ignores the equilibrium (steady-state) salt level \( c_{in}\cdot V \) entirely.
This flips the sign of the transient term, overshooting equilibrium instead of approaching it correctly.
This treats the salt content as changing linearly rather than exponentially toward equilibrium.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.
